title: Chapter 17: Electrical Circuits and Electrical Safety — PYP

questions.question: A resistor converts $350\text{ J}$ of electrical energy when a p.d. of $7\text{ V}$ is applied. What is the amount of charge that flows through it?
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Section A, Question 1. Using $Q = W / V$, $Q = 350 / 7 = 50\text{ C}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying^350 by^7 instead of dividing.

questions.question: The reading on an ammeter in a simple circuit is $1.0\text{ A}$. Which of the following sets of voltmeter reading and resistance is consistent with this current?
questions.explanation:
From Review Question 3. According to $V = IR$, if $I = 1.0\text{ A}$, then $V$ must equal $R$. Only Choice A ($10 = 1 \times 10$) satisfies this. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting Choice D because^12 and^6 are common numbers in electricity problems.

questions.question: A $V-I$ graph for two conductors, A and B, shows that both lines are straight and pass through the origin. Conductor A has a steeper gradient (larger $V$ for the same $I$) than Conductor B. What can be deduced?
options: Conductor A has a higher resistance than Conductor B.
Conductor B has a higher resistance than Conductor A.
Both conductors have the same resistance.
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Question 6. Resistance $R$ is the ratio $V/I$. A steeper gradient on a $V-I$ graph means a higher ratio and thus higher resistance. 

**Common mistake:** Swapping the relationship if the graph is $I-V$ instead of $V-I$.

questions.question: Which circuit set-up cannot be used to determine the resistance of a resistor R?
options: A voltmeter connected in series with the resistor
An ammeter in series and a voltmeter in parallel
A digital multimeter set to the ohmmeter function
Measuring the p.d. and current separately and calculating the ratio
questions.explanation:
From Review Question 2. A voltmeter has very high resistance; if placed in series, it will block almost all current, making it impossible to determine the resistor's normal properties. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking voltmeters can be placed anywhere in a circuit.

questions.question: A student measures the current in a wire to be $0.53\text{ A}$ over a period of $1.0\text{ minute}$. Given the charge of one electron is $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$, how many electrons passed through the wire?
questions.explanation:
From Review Question 4(b). $Q = I \times t = 0.53 \times 60 \approx 32\text{ C}$. Number of electrons $= 32 / (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 2.0 \times 10^{20}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using $1.0$ second as the time instead of^60 seconds.

questions.question: A diagram shows three identical resistors connected in series to a $6\text{ V}$ battery. What is the potential difference between the start of the first resistor and the end of the second resistor?
questions.explanation:
This is based on Let's Review Section A, Question 1. The total $6\text{ V}$ is shared equally among three resistors ($2\text{ V}$ each). Across two resistors, the p.d. is $2 + 2 = 4\text{ V}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which row correctly describes the current and the potential difference in a series circuit with two different resistors?
options: Current: Same at all points; P.d.: Shared between resistors
Current: Shared between resistors; P.d.: Same across each resistor
Current: Same at all points; P.d.: Same across each resistor
Current: Shared between resistors; P.d.: Shared between resistors
questions.explanation:
As stated in the sources, the current in a series circuit is constant at all points, while the total potential difference is equal to the sum of individual p.d.s.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: The p.d. across a $4\text{ }Omega$ resistor connected in series is $2\text{ V}$. If the total potential difference for the whole circuit is $10\text{ V}$, what is the resistance of the rest of the circuit components combined?
questions.explanation:
First, find current: $I = V / R = 2 / 4 = 0.5\text{ A}$. In series, current is the same. The rest of the circuit has p.d. $= 10 - 2 = 8\text{ V}$. Resistance $= V / I = 8 / 0.5 = 16\text{ }Omega$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Three ammeters are placed at different points in a series circuit containing two non-identical resistors. Ammeter 1 reads $0.4\text{ A}$. What do Ammeter 2 and Ammeter 3 read?
options: Ammeter 2 reads $0.2\text{ A}$ and Ammeter 3 reads $0.2\text{ A}$
Ammeter 2 reads $0.4\text{ A}$ and Ammeter 3 reads $0.8\text{ A}$
The readings depend on the values of the resistors
questions.explanation:
By the principle of series circuits, the current is constant throughout the single loop. All ammeters in the same series circuit will provide the same reading.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which graph correctly shows the relationship between total resistance $R_T$ and the number $n$ of identical $5\text{ }Omega$ resistors added in series?
options: A straight line with a positive gradient starting from the origin
A curve that gets steeper as $n$ increases
A straight line with a negative gradient
questions.explanation:
Since $R_T = n \times R_{individual}$, total resistance is directly proportional to the number of identical resistors. This is represented by a straight line through the origin.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: The diagram shows a parallel circuit with three resistors ($4\text{ }Omega, 6\text{ }Omega, 3\text{ }Omega$). The reading on ammeter $A_2$ is $1\text{ A}$ and on $A_4$ (main circuit) is $3\text{ A}$. What are the readings on $A_1$ and $A_3$?nn[Reference: Figure 17.50]
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Q2. The voltage across the $6\text{ }Omega$ resistor is $1\text{ A} \times 6\text{ }Omega = 6\text{ V}$. Current $A_3 = 6\text{ V} / 3\text{ }Omega = 2\text{ A}$. Since $A_4$ is $3\text{ A}$ and $A_2+A_3 = 1+2=3$, the current through $A_1$ must be $0\text{ A}$. 

**Common mistake:** Choosing Choice B, assuming currents are shared equally.

questions.question: A resistor $R_1$ is connected to a $6\text{ V}$ source, and the ammeter reads $2\text{ A}$. A second resistor $R_2$ is then connected in parallel with $R_1$, and the ammeter reading increases to $3\text{ A}$. What is the resistance of $R_2$?
questions.explanation:
From Review Q3. The p.d. across $R_2$ is $6\text{ V}$. The current through $R_2$ is $3\text{ A} - 2\text{ A} = 1\text{ A}$. Resistance $R_2 = 6\text{ V} / 1\text{ A} = 6\text{ }Omega$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the total current ($3\text{ A}$) to find $R_2$ directly ($6/3=2$).

questions.question: Which of the following combinations of resistors ($1\text{ }Omega, 2\text{ }Omega, 3\text{ }Omega, 4\text{ }Omega$) has the lowest combined resistance?
options: A parallel combination of $3\text{ }Omega$ and $4\text{ }Omega$ connected in series with $1\text{ }Omega$
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Q4. The combined resistance of resistors in parallel is always less than the smallest individual resistor in the group. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a complex series-parallel combination could be lower.

questions.question: In a circuit with multiple parallel resistors of equal resistance $R$, which statement correctly relates the source current $I$ and branch currents $I_2$ and $I_3$?nn[Reference: Figure 17.53]
questions.explanation:
From Review Q8. Current $I$ entering the parallel section is the same as current $I_4$ leaving it, and it is the sum of the currents in the individual branches ($I_2$ and $I_3$). 

**Common mistake:** Thinking current magnitude decreases after every junction.

questions.question: A $12\text{ V}$ power source is connected to a parallel combination of a $3\text{ }Omega$ and a $6\text{ }Omega$ resistor. What is the current flowing through the $6\text{ }Omega$ resistor?
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Q1(d). Current $I = V / R = 12 / 6 = 2.0\text{ A}$. 

**Common mistake:** Calculating total resistance ($2\text{ }Omega$) and finding total current ($6\text{ A}$) instead of branch current.

questions.question: The diagram shows a potential divider circuit used to detect the level of sunlight. The resistance of the LDR is $1\text{ M}Omega$ in the dark and $100\text{ }Omega$ in bright sunlight. n[Circuit: $12\text{ V}$ source, $500\text{ k}Omega$ fixed resistor $R_1$, LDR $R_2$, Voltmeter across LDR]nWhat is the approximate voltmeter reading in bright sunlight?
questions.explanation:
In bright sunlight, LDR resistance is only $100\text{ }Omega$. This is negligible compared to $500000\text{ }Omega$. Therefore, the LDR takes almost none of the $12\text{ V}$ supply, and the reading is approximately $0\text{ V}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the relationship between the potential differences and resistances in a series circuit containing two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$?
questions.explanation:
As shown in the potential divider map, in a series circuit, the ratio of the resistances is equal to the ratio of the potential differences across them: $R_1 / R_2 = V_1 / V_2$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A diagram shows three identical resistors connected in series to a $6\text{ V}$ battery. What is the voltage reading of a voltmeter connected across two of the resistors?
questions.explanation:
Since the resistors are identical, each takes $1/3$ of the total voltage ($2\text{ V}$ each). A voltmeter across two resistors will read $2 + 2 = 4\text{ V}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student connects a thermistor in a potential divider circuit. The resistance of this thermistor decreases with an increase in its temperature. What happens to the voltmeter reading across the thermistor when it is heated?
questions.explanation:
When heated, the thermistor's resistance decreases. In a series potential divider, a component with lower resistance takes a smaller share of the total voltage, so the voltmeter reading across it decreases.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A potentiometer with a maximum resistance of $100\text{ }Omega$ is connected to a $6\text{ V}$ supply. The sliding contact is moved from one end (Point A) to the other end (Point B). What is the range of the output voltage $V_{out}$?
questions.explanation:
Based on Worked Example 17F, at Point A the resistance across the output is zero ($V_{out} = 0\text{ V}$). At Point B, the output is across the full resistance ($V_{out} = 6\text{ V}$).

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: An appliance has a normal operating current of $7\text{ A}$. Which fuse rating should be used to protect the appliance in the event of a fault?
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Section A, Question 5. The fuse must be greater than the operating current ($7\text{ A}$), so $13\text{ A}$ is the correct choice. 

**Common mistake:** Choosing a rating lower than the current.

questions.question: Which safety precaution primarily reduces the risk of an electrical fire if a device becomes faulty?
questions.explanation:
Directly from Review Question 6. A trip switch (or fuse) cuts off excessive current, preventing wires from overheating and catching fire. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting earth wire, which mainly prevents electric shock rather than fires.

questions.question: According to the 'Let’s Practise' section, why do some appliances use a three-pin plug while others use a two-pin plug?
options: Three-pin plugs are used for appliances requiring an earth wire; two-pin plugs are for double-insulated appliances.
Two-pin plugs are used for high-voltage industrial machinery.
Three-pin plugs are only used for alternating current (a.c.) devices.
Two-pin plugs include an internal battery for emergency power.
questions.explanation:
Based on Practise 17.5, Question 3. Double-insulated (plastic case) devices don't need earthing, while metal-cased ones do. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the number of pins is related to the voltage level.

questions.question: An electrician uses a voltmeter to test a faulty circuit. The table shows the readings. What is the most likely reason for the $0\text{ V}$ reading between points B and C?nn| Points | P.d. across points |n| :--- | :--- |n| A and B | $40\text{ V}$ |n| B and C | $0\text{ V}$ |n| D and E | $100\text{ V}$ |
options: There is no resistance between points B and C.
The circuit is broken (open) between B and C.
The fuse has blown at point B.
The potential difference is being absorbed by the earth wire.
questions.explanation:
From Review Question 4. According to $V = IR$, if resistance is zero, the potential difference across that section will also be zero. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a $0\text{ V}$ reading means the 'electricity is off' entirely.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies the function of each wire in a mains supply?nn| | Live Wire | Neutral Wire | Earth Wire |n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |n| A | Carries high potential | Completes the circuit | Safety path |n| B | Safety path | Completes the circuit | Carries high potential |n| C | Completes the circuit | Carries high potential | Safety path |n| D | Carries high potential | Safety path | Completes the circuit |
questions.explanation:
The live wire provides the alternating potential, the neutral wire completes the loop, and the earth wire is for safety. 

**Common mistake:** Swapping the functions of the neutral and earth wires.
